content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 02, 2017

Sue Sorensen
Hanford City Councilmember
1369 W. Dominic
Hanford, CA 93230

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-076

Dear Ms. Sorensen:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Additionally, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTIONS

1. Does your ownership interest in a partnership that owns a professional office building in the downtown area create a conflict of interest limiting your participation in the decision to adopt the proposed Zoning Ordinance amendments related to the City of Hanford's 2035 General Plan update?

2. May you participate in future actions to promote the downtown area?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The decision to adopt the proposed Zoning Ordinance related to the 2035 General Plan update will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your interests and you may not participate in the decision.

2. The actions to promote the downtown area are not specific enough at this time for us to provide an analysis. Please contact us for further assistance when you have additional information.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-076
Page No. 2


FACTS

You were elected to the Hanford City Council in November 2016 and took office in
December 2106. You and your spouse own a 12.5% interest in "The Laundry Building," a
partnership that owns a professional office building located in historic downtown Hanford. The
downtown area is a small fraction of non-residential property located in the City. Your property is
zoned Downtown Commercial ("DC") and is leased to commercial tenants: a hair salon, dental lab,
dentist, and an insurance agency. The property has an approximate value of two to three million
dollars, and the partnership has invested a "significant amount" in improving and maintaining the
property.

Under the 2002 General Plan, the City encouraged the vitality of its historic downtown
district through zoning restrictions on other districts. These zoning ordinances limit or centralize
commercial uses such as: furniture sales, banks, movie theaters, and professional offices to the DC
zoning district. You state that when the General Plan was adopted in 2002 the City's intent was to
have Planned Commercial (PC) zone districts "not compete with the downtown area." The 2002
General Plan states:

It is the City's intent to preserve the downtown as a viable commercial center. The
Planned Commercial districts provide for retail commercial areas outside of the
downtown commercial district of the city. These areas will be within a unified
shopping center complex to serve the community. Several major anchor tenants,
along with supporting uses, would be expected at a Planned Commercial center.
Specifically excluded from the PC areas are financial uses, furniture stores,
professional offices and professional service uses, commercial offices and
commercial service uses.

(General Plan Adopted June 18, 2002, Land Use Element, p. LU-12.)

The City voted to update its general plan with the proposed 2035 General Plan on April 24,
2017 and is preparing to vote on the corresponding Zoning Ordinance amendments on May 2, 2017.
You have recused yourself from proceedings due to your interest in the commercial property.

You state that the proposed Zoning Ordinance eliminates or eases many aspects of the City's
restrictive zoning set forth in the 2002 General Plan and corresponding Zoning Ordinance. For
example, the proposed Zoning Ordinance would now allow "Office; medical, dental or optometry"
and "Office; professional or commercial" in the "C-R" zone (former PC and Community
Commercial) under a conditional use permit. (City Municipal Code Title 17-Zoning Ordinance,
Draft March 2017, Table 17-08.030, p. 23.)²

² The 2035 General Plan proposes new land use designations; the PC and "Community Commercial" will be
"Regional Commercial" or "C-R". (General Plan Update, p. 27.) The DC zone is will be "Downtown Mixed Use" or
"MX-D". (General Plan Update, Table 3-4, p. 27.)

File No. A-17-076
Page No. 3

These proposals have given rise to public concern and specifically to "Main Street
Hanford," a private non-profit group of downtown business owners, that this increased competition
for downtown business may result in downtown business failures and resulting blight in the area.
You note that several downtown business owners who have "spent considerable amounts to
improve their respective buildings" in the historic downtown area are concerned about the impact of
the proposed decision. To address these concerns the City will be considering other measures to
protect the viability of the historic downtown area. These measures may include incentives and
financial assistance, but there are no specific decisions currently at issue. You anticipate that the
City Council will address possible protective measures for the downtown area at a later date.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material, and distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.

Section 87103 defines financial interests to include:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(b)), including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including any
community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of
any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)).

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

You have a financial interest in:

• The Laundry Building partnership as a business entity and source of income;

• In the commercial building as real property in which you and your spouse own a 12.5%
interest.

File No. A-17-076
Page No. 4

• You also have a financial interest in any tenant of the commercial building as a source of income, where your pro rata share of income from any tenant aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to a decision.

To determine if you have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act we determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on your business and real property interests. (Regulation 18700(d).)

Foreseeability

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly" or "not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) A financial interest is explicitly involved when it is a named party or the" subject" of the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)

Real property, such as yours, is the "subject" when the decision involves items (1) through (6) in Regulation 18702.2 (a), the materiality standard for real property:

(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as provided below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries of the plan;

(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or de-annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other local government subdivision, or other boundaries,. . . .

(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel;

(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel;

(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on, that real property. . . .

(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision;

(Regulation 18701(a) and Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) -(6).)

File No. A-17-076
Page No. 5

Your facts do not meet the requirements of any of these provisions (items (1) through (6)).
Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability standard is
"whether the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical
or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Materiality

Real Property: To determine materiality, we look to Regulation 18702.2(a), items (7)
through (12), with the following most relevant to these facts:

. . .

(8) Would change the income producing potential of the parcel of real property.
However, if the real property contains a business entity, including rental property,
and the nature of the business entity remains unchanged, the materiality standards
under Regulation 18702.1 applicable to business entities would apply instead;

. . .

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of
the official's property.

As directed in Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), we must examine the materiality standards in
Regulation 18702.1 relating to business entities. Applicable to the facts here, Regulation
18702.1(b)(2) states:

(b) For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a
privately-held business entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable
include those that:

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business entity is engaged;

The zoning ordinance amendments related to the 2035 General Plan update would lift the
current restrictions on professional office space outside of the DC zoning area and increase the
amount of competition in the professional office space market. "Office; medical, dental or
optometry" and "Office; professional or commercial" would be permissible in the "C-R" zone with
a conditional use permit. Under this decision your tenants, which include dental and professional
offices, will likely have more choices for leasing space in the City. It is already anticipated by the
public that these amendments will increase competition to the level that it "may result in downtown
business failures and blight." Downtown business owners that have invested in their historic
commercial buildings are "concerned" about the impact this will have on the vitality of the historic

File No. A-17-076
Page No. 6

downtown. It is also reasonable that your tenants, as a source of income, may face additional competition from businesses that locate in the C-R zone.³ Based on these facts, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions could affect the rental income of the partnership's building, the value of your partnership interest, and your tenants as a source of income. Therefore, you have a prohibited conflict of interest and may not make, participate, or use your position to influence this decision.⁴

New Measures to Protect the Historic Downtown Area:

These measures are anticipated; however, you have provided no specific decisions to address or facts to analyze. Please request additional advice once you have additional information.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

³ Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a source of income, however it provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is also determined pursuant to the standards of Regulation 18702.1. See the above paragraph for the analysis and discussion of Regulation 18702.1.

⁴ The "public generally" exception is not applicable, as you indicate the downtown area is "a small fraction of non-residential property located in the City." (See Regulation 18703.)

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material, and distinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. 
Section 87103 defines financial interests to include: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b)), including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent interest or greater (Section 82033). 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to 
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including any 
community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of 
any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)). 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
You have a financial interest in: 
• The Laundry Building partnership as a business entity and source of income; 
• In the commercial building as real property in which you and your spouse own a 12.5% 
interest.  
• You also have a financial interest in any tenant of the commercial building as a source of 
income, where your pro rata share of income from any tenant aggregates to $500 or more in 
the 12 months prior to a decision. 
To determine if you have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act we determine 
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on your 
business and real property interests. (Regulation l 8700(d).) 
Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly" or 
"not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly 
involved, a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) A financial interest is 
explicitly involved when it is a named party or the" subject" of the decision. (Regulation 1870 l (a).) 
Real property, such as yours, is the "subject" when the decision involves items (l) through 
(6) in Regulation 18702.2 (a), the materiality standard for real property: 
( 1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general ( except as provided 
below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries 
of the plan; 
(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning decision 
applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or de­
annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other 
local government subdivision, or other boundaries, . . .  
(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to 
the parcel; 
(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel; 
(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land 
use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any 
variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on, that real 
property . . . .  
(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm 
drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest 
will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements 
and services that are provided to or received by other similarly situated properties 
in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise receive a 
disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision; 
(Regulation 1870l(a) and Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) -(6).)  
Your facts do not meet the requirements of any of these provisions (items (I) through (6)). 
Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability standard is 
"whether the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality 
Real Property: To determine materiality, we look to Regulation 18702.2(a), items (7) 
through (12), with the following most relevant to these facts: 
(8) Would change the income producing potential of the parcel of real property. 
However, if the real property contains a business entity, including rental property, 
and the nature of the business entity remains unchanged, the materiality standards 
under Regulation 18702.1 applicable to business entities would apply instead; 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
As directed in Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), we must examine the materiality standards in 
Regulation 18702.1 relating to business entities. Applicable to the facts here, Regulation 
18702.1 (b )(2) states: 
(b) For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial 
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a 
privately-held business entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable 
include those that: 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
The zoning ordinance amendments related to the 2035 General Plan update would lift the 
current restrictions on professional office space outside of the DC zoning area and increase the 
amount of competition in the professional office space market. "Office; medical, dental or 
optometry" and "Office; professional or commercial" would be permissible in the ''C-R" zone with 
a conditional use permit. Under this decision your tenants, which include dental and professional 
offices, will likely have more choices for leasing space in the City. It is already anticipated by the 
public that these amendments will increase competition to the level that it "may result in downtown 
business failures and blight." Downtown business owners that have invested in their historic 
commercial buildings are "concerned" about the impact this will have on the vitality of the historic  
downtown. It is also reasonable that your tenants, as a source of income, may face additional 
competition from businesses that locate in the C-R zone. 3 Based on these facts, it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decisions could affect the rental income of the partnership's building, the value 
of your partnership interest, and your tenants as a source of income. Therefore, you have a 
prohibited conflict of interest and may not make, participate, or use your position to influence this 
decision.4 
New Measures to Protect the Historic Downtown Area: 
These measures are anticipated; however, you have provided no specific decisions to
